planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring, or in late summer 6–8 weeks before first fall frost; red leaf lettuces are loose-head types harvested as cut-and-come-again crops by removing outer leaves as needed, or as whole heads — heat bolts them quickly so sow again in early fall for best flavor.

directions: Sow seed thinly on the surface and press in gently; lettuce seeds need light to germinate and should not be covered more than 1/8 inch deep; keep moist until germination. Red leaf lettuce encompasses looseleaf types with varying degrees of red-to-burgundy pigmentation in the leaves, including classics like 'Lollo Rossa,' 'New Red Fire,' 'Red Sails,' and 'Oak Red' — the anthocyanin pigmentation develops best in cool temperatures and direct sunlight, with heat causing color to fade and bitterness to increase. Red leaf lettuces are the most commonly grown looseleaf types in American home gardens, valued for their visual appeal, nutritional density (the red pigment is anthocyanins with antioxidant value), and ease of growing as cut-and-come-again salad plants. Harvest outer leaves when 4–6 inches long, leaving the center to continue growing; provide shade cloth (30%) in warm climates to extend the season. Interplant with taller vegetables for natural afternoon shade.
sources: North Carolina State Extension Vegetable Production Guide

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for spring harvest; oak leaf lettuce is distinguished by its distinctive deeply lobed, oak leaf-shaped leaves that make it one of the most heat-tolerant looseleaf lettuces and among the slowest to bolt in warm conditions — an excellent choice for spring and fall production in zones 8–11.

directions: Scatter seed thinly on the soil surface and firm in; keep consistently moist until germination at 50–70°F. Oak Leaf lettuce is one of the most recognizable and popular looseleaf lettuces in American home gardens, with deeply lobed, ruffled leaves that resemble the silhouette of an oak leaf — a form distinct from the curled frill of Lollo Rosa types or the smooth curves of Butterhead. The bright green (or burgundy, in the 'Red Oak Leaf' variety) deeply cut leaves form loose, open rosettes that can be cut-and-come-again repeatedly; the leaf texture is tender and buttery, and the flavor is mild with excellent heat tolerance compared to most lettuces. It was one of Thomas Jefferson's preferred salad plants at Monticello and has been grown in American gardens continuously since the colonial era. Outstanding for cut-and-come-again salad production in spring and fall.
sources: Monticello Heritage Harvest Festival Seed List

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for spring harvest; Little Gem is a miniature romaine-butterhead cross that produces small, compact, dense heads of exceptional quality — the 'sucrine' of French cuisine, considered by many the most flavorful lettuce for whole-head salads and prized by restaurant chefs for its combination of crunch and buttery flavor.

directions: Sow on the soil surface and press in gently; germinate at 50–70°F. Little Gem is a miniature romaine/butterhead cross producing small (4–5 inch), compact, upright heads with firm, crunchy outer leaves and a sweet, butter-cream interior heart — essentially a mini Caesar salad head in one plant. Also known as 'Sucrine' in France (from the French for 'sugar'), it has become extremely popular in restaurant and farmers market contexts as a whole-head salad lettuce grilled or served halved with dressings. The outer leaves have romaine's crunch while the hearts are as sweet and buttery as buttercrunch — the best of both types in a small, space-efficient package. 4-6 inch spacing allows intensive planting; harvest the whole head when firm and compact. More heat-tolerant than standard butterhead and holds well in the garden without bolting quickly.

planting.note: Sow in late summer 8–10 weeks before first fall frost for a fall-into-winter harvest; Rouge d'Hiver is a French heirloom romaine lettuce bred specifically for cold weather production, surviving temperatures well below freezing and producing elongated, bronzy-red to burgundy heads through winter and early spring in zones 6–10.

directions: Sow in late summer in rich, moist, well-drained soil and thin to 9–12 inches; Rouge d'Hiver tolerates hard frost and is best grown as a fall-to-winter crop in mild climates, or with cold frame protection in colder zones. Rouge d'Hiver (Red Winter) is a French heirloom romaine-type lettuce bred for extreme cold tolerance — a critical trait in French kitchen garden tradition where gardeners have long maintained winter salad production under cloches and frames. The upright, elongated, semi-cos heads develop deep bronzy-red to burgundy coloring that intensifies in cold weather, with firm, crunchy texture and rich, slightly nutty flavor that is superior to most warm-season lettuces. In zones 8–10 it can be grown outdoors through winter without protection; in zones 6–7 it thrives in cold frames or low tunnels. One of the most valuable and underused lettuces for Gulf Coast and Southeastern winter salad gardens where it will produce from October through March.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in spring; Bibb lettuce is a Kentucky heirloom developed by Jack Bibb of Frankfort around 1865, a butterhead type producing small, loose, cupped heads of buttery, sweet, tender leaves — the definitive American butterhead lettuce and considered by many the most flavorful lettuce for salads.

directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface in early spring in fertile, well-drained, consistently moist soil; thin to 9–12 inches and harvest the whole head when the center begins to feel firm. Bibb lettuce was developed by amateur horticulturist Jack Bibb of Frankfort, Kentucky around 1865 and was distributed to neighbors and friends for decades before entering commercial seed trade; it is a butterhead-type lettuce producing small, loose, semi-cupped heads of exceptionally tender, silky, buttery leaves with a mild, sweet, distinctive flavor that many consider the finest of any lettuce for raw salads. The original Limestone Bibb (named for the limestone water of Kentucky) is more delicate and refined than commercial butterhead varieties but slightly slower and less uniform; it is the 'real' Bibb for flavor purists. Modern hybrid butterhead varieties ('Boston,' 'Summer Bibb,' etc.) are improvements in yield and bolt-resistance but lack some of the exceptional flavor of the true heritage Bibb.
sources: Weaver, William Woys. Heirloom Vegetable Gardening

common_name: Winter Density Lettuce (British Cold-Hardy Romaine-Butterhead Cross)

planting.note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost in spring or in late summer for fall harvest; Winter Density is the quintessential British cold-season lettuce — a Romaine/Butterhead cross with tight, upright heads, exceptionally cold-hardy (tolerating temperatures to 22°F), and slow-bolting even in warming spring conditions; outstanding for year-round production in mild climates and cold frames in zones 5–9.

directions: Sow directly 1/4 inch deep in cool weather; Winter Density tolerates more cold than most lettuces and can be direct-sown in early spring (4–6 weeks before last frost) or late summer for fall harvest; thin to 8–10 inches apart; harvest as baby lettuce at 3–4 weeks or allow heads to mature fully; the heads hold in the garden well without bolting even in warm spring weather. Winter Density is a British heirloom lettuce representing a distinct type: a cross between the upright, crisp Romaine (Cos) type and the loose, buttery Butterhead type, producing compact, upright heads with the outer leaf structure and dark green color of Romaine but the soft, buttery, pale interior leaves and gentle flavor of Butterhead. The flavor is excellent — sweet, slightly nutty, and mild — while the cold tolerance (to around 22°F without protection) and slow-bolting behavior make it one of the most productive lettuces for extended spring seasons and for winter harvests in mild climates (zones 7–10). It is notably more shade-tolerant than most lettuces, performing well under tall vegetables in summer where direct-sun types would bolt. Winter Density was widely grown in English kitchen gardens throughout the 19th and 20th centuries as a reliable, all-conditions lettuce, and has been preserved through seed-saving networks and specialty seed companies.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring through early summer (Jericho tolerates warmer soil and air temperatures than most lettuces), or in fall; this variety was developed in Israel for the extreme heat of the Jordan Valley — the name references Jericho, one of the hottest inhabited cities on earth — and is the premier heat-tolerant Romaine for gardeners who want salad lettuce through warm spring and early summer when other Romaine varieties bolt rapidly.

directions: Sow directly 1/4 inch deep; Jericho tolerates warmer soil and air temperatures than most lettuces, extending the spring sowing season by 2–4 weeks beyond the window suitable for other varieties; provide afternoon shade in the hottest weather; thin to 8–12 inches; harvest full heads or use as cut-and-come-again. Jericho is an Israeli-developed Romaine (Cos) type lettuce bred specifically for the extreme heat of the Jordan Valley and the Mediterranean climate of Israel, where summer temperatures regularly exceed 100°F and conventional lettuce bolts immediately. The variety was developed to remain productive longer into warm weather than any other Romaine, and delivers this through exceptional heat tolerance — it is typically the last lettuce still producing usable heads when spring heat arrives in mid-latitude gardens, and one of the earliest candidates for late-summer planting when other varieties would bolt immediately. The large, upright heads have crisp, pale green Romaine-style leaves with excellent flavor — sweet, slightly nutty, and refreshing — and the heads are notably larger than many other heat-tolerant varieties. For Gulf Coast gardeners (zones 8b–10), Jericho is the most valuable Romaine for the challenging transition from cool-season winter lettuce into warming spring, providing 2–4 extra weeks of production compared to standard varieties. Highly recommended by Territorial Seed and Johnny's for hot-summer and transition-zone gardens.

common_name: Merveille des Quatre Saisons Lettuce (Marvel of Four Seasons, French Red Butterhead)

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring through early summer (succession sow every 3 weeks), or in late summer for fall harvest; Merveille des Quatre Saisons (French for 'Marvel of Four Seasons') is one of the most beautiful French butterhead heirlooms, producing bicolor heads of deep red-bronze outer leaves surrounding creamy pale-yellow inner leaves — a centerpiece of the classic French kitchen garden for more than a century.

directions: Sow 1/4 inch deep and thin to 10–12 inches apart; Merveille des Quatre Saisons produces its most stunning red coloration when nights are cool (45–55°F); succession sow every 3 weeks for continuous harvest; harvest full heads by cutting at the base or harvest outer leaves as cut-and-come-again. Merveille des Quatre Saisons is a classic French butterhead heirloom lettuce with roots in the traditional potager (kitchen garden) of France, where it has been grown and selected for generations as both an ornamental and culinary variety of exceptional beauty. The heads are distinctly bicolor: the outer leaves are deep crimson to mahogany-bronze with the red intensifying dramatically in cold weather, while the inner leaves are creamy pale yellow-green — the contrast between brilliant red outer leaves and golden interior makes this one of the most photographed and admired lettuces in the garden. The 'quatre saisons' (four seasons) name reflects its traditional use throughout the growing year in French kitchen gardens. The flavor is excellent: sweet, buttery, and mild with the silky tenderness characteristic of well-grown butterhead types. The name and variety appear in French seed catalogs dating to at least the 1880s, making it among the oldest continuously cultivated lettuce varieties still widely available.
